zones.name: Build & Release Scripts
zones.description: Provides the complete build, packaging, distribution, and architectural validation pipeline for GoToBed as a set of standalone shell scripts invoked by the Makefile and CI workflows.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 8 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)

Two complementary architectural guard scripts operate at different layers: check-core-purity.sh enforces module-level UI framework exclusion from GoToBedCore, while check-zone-layering.sh enforces intra-module type-reference isolation within GoToBedKit — both must run in CI to prevent silent layering erosion

The app signing and DMG packaging flow is intentionally sequential: build-app.sh produces a code-signed universal binary (accepting an optional NOTARY_PROFILE), then make-dmg.sh wraps it into a distributable DMG with the same notarization path — callers must invoke them in order

stamp-site-version.sh automates version stamping for site docs; confirm it reads from the VERSION file or built bundle rather than deriving a version independently, since release-please and VERSION already own that source of truth

check-site-docs.sh validates docs/llms.txt against Packaging/Info.plist, creating the only runtime bridge between the static-site-assets zone and the packaging zone — this cross-zone consistency check is the right place to catch docs drift on LSMinimumSystemVersion changes.

verify-no-network.sh enforces network isolation during builds or tests to protect reproducibility; confirm it is wired into the Makefile test or build targets so it cannot be bypassed when running make directly.

zones.name: Log Overlaycontroller
insights: High coupling (0.79) — 6 imports target "sources"
High coupling (0.79) — 6 imports target "sources"

OverlayController has no Store dependency and receives Schedule exclusively via the present() callback — the zone's high coupling (0.79) is carried entirely by SchedulerEngine's Store subscription, not by OverlayController. The two services have fundamentally different coupling profiles despite sharing a zone.

Log.swift imports only Foundation and os — it has zero application-layer dependencies and contributes nothing to the zone's coupling score. Its placement here is a Louvain artifact; structurally it belongs at the domain foundation level alongside GoToBedCore.

OverlayController is a one-way sink: it receives Schedule via present() and has no back-channel to Store or SchedulerEngine. The bidirectional coupling reported between log-overlaycontroller ↔ sources is driven by SchedulerEngine alone, not by both services equally — splitting them into separate zones would accurately reflect this asymmetry.

Log.swift (pure Foundation/os utility, no application imports) is grouped with two high-coupling runtime services by community detection. Its inclusion distorts cohesion measurements for the zone without reflecting any actual structural relationship.

zones.name: Sources
insights: Zone "sources" has files across 8 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

AppEnvironment.swift (in this zone) is explicitly documented as the composition root — it wires Store → SchedulerEngine → OverlayController in its initializer and uses a closure to deliver Schedule from scheduler.onFire to overlay.present(). This single file is the origin of the bidirectional coupling between 'sources' ↔ 'sources-gotobed' and 'sources' ↔ 'log-overlaycontroller': AppEnvironment imports both execution-tier services and UI components while those components import domain models back through GoToBedCore.

The store field on AppEnvironment is declared public (line 16), directly exposing the mutable Store to any consumer that holds an AppEnvironment reference. Settings-ui views that go through AppEnvironment coordinator methods are correctly shielded, but any code that accesses env.store directly bypasses the write-proxy contract described in AppEnvironment's own documentation.

AppEnvironment uses a static shared singleton (line 14) as its access point. This means the composition root is globally reachable from any file that imports the module, not just from the app entry point — the intended constraint (only AppEnvironment decides when and how the model changes) is unenforced by the type system.

The bidirectional coupling between 'sources' and 'sources-gotobed' is a composition-root artifact: AppEnvironment (sources) imports SettingsWindowController and other UI components (sources-gotobed) while those components import GoToBedCore models. Extracting the composition wiring into a dedicated AppDelegate or SceneDelegate file would make the coupling directional and eliminate the zone-level bidirectionality.

zones.name: Sources Gotobed
insights: Zone "sources-gotobed" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

MenuContent reads ScheduleDisplayItem (a computed property on AppEnvironment) rather than raw Schedule domain types. This provides a deliberate view-model translation layer that prevents Schedule fields irrelevant to the menu UI (e.g. dismissMode timing, appearance colour components) from leaking into presentation logic.

MenuContent's indirection through ScheduleDisplayItem is the only domain-to-UI translation layer in the codebase. OverlayView and OverlayController both consume the raw Schedule struct directly. If Schedule gains fields that are internal to domain logic, OverlayView will receive them with no filtering — the same ScheduleDisplayItem pattern applied to the overlay path would make the boundary consistent.

zones.name: Static Site Assets
zones.description: Hosts the public-facing GoToBed documentation site assets including SEO metadata, PWA manifest, and AI-readable project summary for the GitHub Pages site.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 5 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
llms.txt is validated against Packaging/Info.plist by scripts/check-site-docs.sh — any LSMinimumSystemVersion bump in Info.plist must be reflected here or CI will fail
The CNAME, robots.txt, and sitemap.xml form the complete SEO and domain-binding surface; all three should be reviewed together whenever the distribution domain changes
site.webmanifest provides PWA configuration for the static documentation site, not the native macOS app — this is the expected pattern for a GitHub Pages hosted docs site
Zero cohesion and coupling scores reflect the structural absence of import relationships among static web assets — this is correct and expected, not a decay signal.

zones.name: Tests Gotobedcoretests
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

zones.name: Tests Gotobedtests
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Hub: Sources/GoToBedCore/Models/Schedule.swift is imported by 4 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Hub: Sources/GoToBedCore/Models/Validation.swift is imported by 4 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Hub: Sources/GoToBedCore/Store/Store.swift is imported by 3 zones — cross-cutting dependency
Bidirectional coupling: "sources" ↔ "sources-gotobed" (11+8 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "log-overlaycontroller" ↔ "sources" (6+8 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
4 circular dependency chains detected — see imports.json for details
The scripts and docs zones are both structurally isolated with zero import coupling to any source zone — build infrastructure and static assets correctly sit outside the application dependency graph

The two architectural guard scripts (check-core-purity.sh, check-zone-layering.sh) complement Package.swift target boundaries: Package.swift enforces module separation at compile time while the shell scripts make the same constraints machine-checkable at the grep level, providing defense-in-depth for core-purity and zone-layering invariants

The cross-zone import graph shows a clean directed topology: test zones import source zones, source zones import each other in a DAG, and neither scripts nor docs participate — confirming infrastructure zones are correctly decoupled from the application import graph

The scripts zone combines build automation (build-app.sh, make-dmg.sh, make-icon.sh) with architectural enforcement scripts (check-core-purity.sh, check-zone-layering.sh, verify-no-network.sh) in a flat directory — these serve different audiences but the naming convention makes their purpose clear without further subdivision.
File "Tests/GoToBedCoreTests/SubmessageTests.swift" has 100% of import neighbors in Sources/GoToBedCore/Models/ but lives in Tests/GoToBedCoreTests/ — consider relocating

All inter-zone coupling ultimately terminates at AppEnvironment.swift — it is both the composition root and the source of every bidirectional zone relationship. The 4 circular dependency chains reported by sourcevision are composition-root-induced: AppEnvironment (sources) imports execution-tier services (log-overlaycontroller) and UI components (sources-gotobed) while those components import domain models that live in the same 'sources' zone, closing apparent cycles at the zone graph level without any true import circularity at the file level.

The GoToBedCore models (Schedule, Validation, Store) are hub files with zero upward dependencies — they import only Foundation and Combine. Their hub status in the import graph is a sign of a healthy domain layer, not a design smell: cross-cutting domain types are expected to be widely imported.

The scheduler-to-overlay data path is implemented entirely via closure injection (scheduler.onFire = { overlay.present($0) } in AppEnvironment.init), making it invisible to static import analysis. This explains why sourcevision shows no direct edge from SchedulerEngine to OverlayController despite them being functionally coupled at runtime.

The runtime data flow (Store mutation → SchedulerEngine re-arms → onFire closure → OverlayController.present) is fully acyclic and unidirectional, but the zone-level import graph appears bidirectional because the composition root (AppEnvironment) sits in the same Louvain zone as the domain models it depends on. Separating AppEnvironment into its own zone (or into the app entry-point target) would make the zone graph match the runtime topology.
